\toprule
\textbf{Method} & $\sigma=0$ & $\sigma=2$ & $\sigma=4$ & \textbf{Increase} \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{London, UK (Summer)}} \\
Oracle          & \$18.09 & \$18.09 & \$18.09 & +0.0\% \\
MPC             & \$18.43 & \$18.14 & \$20.01 & +8.5\% \\
AIF Independent & \$18.61 & \$18.41 & \$20.17 & +8.4\% \\
AIF Aligned     & \$18.61 & \$18.41 & \$20.17 & +8.4\% \\
AIF Federated   & \$21.34 & \$21.34 & \$20.32 & $-$4.8\% \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{5}{l}{\textit{Phoenix, AZ (Summer)}} \\
Oracle          & \$19.65 & \$19.65 & \$19.65 & +0.0\% \\
MPC             & \$19.70 & \$20.19 & \$20.45 & +3.8\% \\
AIF Independent & \$20.44 & \$20.44 & \$20.44 & +0.0\% \\
AIF Aligned     & \$20.44 & \$20.44 & \$20.44 & +0.0\% \\
AIF Federated   & \$20.37 & \$20.37 & \$20.37 & +0.0\% \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The results highlight key robustness differences. In Phoenix, where cooling runs near-continuously,
AIF costs are completely invariant to forecast noise: the agents
maintain \$20.44 from $\sigma=0$ through $\sigma=4$ (0.0\% increase).
MPC, by contrast, degrades by 3.8\% as it misallocates battery resources
based on incorrect temperature predictions. In London, both AIF and MPC
degrade similarly at high noise levels (+8.4\% and +8.5\% respectively),
reflecting London's more variable thermal load where forecast errors
affect HVAC scheduling. The Federated mode shows an anomalous decrease
in London (--4.8\%), likely because noisy forecasts occasionally trigger
more conservative battery scheduling that happens to align with the
actual ToU pattern.

Independent and Aligned produce identical results across all conditions,
confirming that both modes now use the same ToU-aware dynamic $C$
vectors and differ only in whether inter-agent coordination mechanisms
(belief sharing, phantom inference) are enabled. The Oracle's invariance
(+0.0\% by definition) provides a lower bound, and AIF in Phoenix
achieves this bound---the agents' generative models are robust enough
that temperature forecast errors do not affect their battery scheduling
decisions when HVAC demand is dominated by cooling.

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\begin{centering}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\columnwidth]{figures/fig19_uncertainty_robustness}
\par\end{centering}
\caption{Percentage cost increase relative to zero-noise baseline
($\sigma=0$) as forecast noise increases. AIF agents in Phoenix show
zero degradation, while MPC degrades by 3.8\%.}\label{fig:uncertainty_robustness}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\begin{centering}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\columnwidth]{figures/fig19_uncertainty_absolute}
\par\end{centering}
\caption{Absolute weekly cost as a function of forecast noise level.
Oracle (constant by definition) provides the lower bound.
AIF costs in Phoenix remain flat across all noise levels.}\label{fig:uncertainty_absolute}
\end{figure}


\subsection{Reinforcement Learning Comparison}\label{sec:rl_comparison}

To contextualize AIF performance, we compare against improved tabular
Q-learning baselines with both the original (10-bin) and improved
(34-bin) state discretizations, trained for 500 to 20,000 episodes.
Three AIF reference methods are included: Aligned, Sophisticated
(symmetric phantoms), and Full Sophisticated (expanded policy space).
Three climates (London summer, Montreal winter, Phoenix summer) are
tested with 3 random seeds each.

Figure~\ref{fig:rl_convergence} shows convergence curves for both RL
discretizations alongside the AIF reference lines.
Table~\ref{tab:rl_convergence} summarizes the key comparisons.

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption{RL convergence summary: cost gap relative to Oracle for the
best RL configuration (20,000 episodes) vs.\ AIF Aligned.
All values averaged over 3 seeds.}\label{tab:rl_convergence}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Metric} & \textbf{London} & \textbf{Montreal} & \textbf{Phoenix} \\
\midrule
Oracle cost      & \$18.09 & \$19.09 & \$19.65 \\
AIF Aligned gap  & +2.9\%  & +1.3\%  & +4.0\%  \\
RL-20k gap       & +26.2\% & $-$2.2\%  & +14.5\% \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

AIF Aligned achieves costs within 2.9--4.0\% of the Oracle across all
three climates with zero training episodes, while the best RL agent
(34-bin, 20,000 episodes) remains 14.5--26.2\% above Oracle in London
and Phoenix. In Montreal winter, where the extreme cold forces near-continuous
heating, RL achieves a modest advantage ($-$2.2\% below Oracle cost),
likely because the simplified Q-learning policy happens to match the
dominant ``always heat'' strategy. However, this comes at the cost of
severely degraded comfort (4026.9~$^{\circ}$C$\cdot$h in the
Section~\ref{sec:cost_aware_results} results).

\textbf{Cross-climate transfer.} A key advantage of model-based approaches
is generalization without retraining. We train RL on London summer
(10,000 episodes) and evaluate on Phoenix summer without retraining.
Table~\ref{tab:rl_transfer} shows the results.

\begin{table}[htbp]
\centering
\caption{Cross-climate transfer: RL trained on London summer and
evaluated on Phoenix summer (no retraining) vs.\ AIF which requires
no training.}\label{tab:rl_transfer}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Method} & \textbf{London} & \textbf{Phoenix} \\
\midrule
Oracle                     & \$18.09 $\pm$ 0.69 & \$19.65 $\pm$ 0.49 \\
AIF Aligned (zero training)& \$18.61 $\pm$ 0.69 & \$20.44 $\pm$ 0.44 \\
RL (trained on London)     & \$22.21 $\pm$ 3.78 & \$25.95 $\pm$ 2.72 \\
RL (trained on Phoenix)    & ---                & \$23.22 $\pm$ 2.37 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

RL trained on London degrades by 11.8\% when transferred to Phoenix,
reflecting overfitting to London's thermal dynamics and ToU patterns.
Even RL trained \emph{on} Phoenix (\$23.22) underperforms AIF (\$20.44),
and exhibits substantially higher variance ($\pm$\$2.37 vs.\
$\pm$\$0.44). AIF achieves 0\% transfer degradation because its
generative model adapts online through belief updating rather than
relying on pre-trained policies.

\begin{figure}[htbp]
\begin{centering}
\includegraphics[width=0.85\columnwidth]{figures/fig20_rl_convergence}
\par\end{centering}
\caption{RL convergence curves for original (10-bin) and improved
(34-bin) Q-learning across three climates. Dashed horizontal lines show
Oracle, AIF Aligned, and AIF Sophisticated reference costs. Even at
20,000 training episodes, RL does not reach AIF-level performance
in London or Phoenix.}\label{fig:rl_convergence}
\end{figure}


\section{Discussion}

As societal electrification increases in response to climate change,
and as the penetration of DER devices in distribution grids increases,
it becomes increasingly important for grid flexibility and stability
not only that each energy device is intelligently managed, but that
management is coordinated between devices within a household, between
households of a neighborhood or complex, and across regions of the
grid. Intelligent management involves forecasts (of weather, solar
generation, energy use, household occupancy, etc.) and such forecasts
involve uncertainty. Related, the thermodynamic model of a house is
typically unknown and must be estimated, which also involves uncertainty.
Thus, the use of multi-agent probabilistic models for HEMS appears
promising. In this, the use of active inference seems particularly
promising, as an active inference agent can choose actions to resolve
uncertainty. In an advanced model for example, an agent might ask
a householder if a room will be occupied later in the evening, if
it is unsure. Relatively few HEMS studies conducted so far consider
probabilistic models, however.

Here we demonstrate a proof-of-concept active inference model. Results
suggest that the model is able to plan ahead to balance conflicting
preferences for energy use, energy cost, greenhouse gas emissions,
and comfort. The multi-seed Oracle gap analysis
(Section~\ref{sec:cost_aware_results}) provides the most rigorous
evaluation: across four climate scenarios and five random seeds,
all AIF agents achieve costs within 2.0\% of the perfect-foresight
Oracle on average (maximum gap 4.2\% in London summer), with the cost
advantage over a fixed-thermostat baseline reaching 17\% in mild climates.
This near-optimal performance arises from ToU-aware dynamic $C$ vectors
and predictive $B$ matrices that enable the agent to anticipate
environmental changes within its planning horizon. MPC with a 6-step horizon achieves near-Oracle performance,
confirming that the planning problem is well-conditioned when given
sufficient lookahead; the advantage of AIF lies in its probabilistic
framework and uncertainty quantification rather than in raw performance.
All AIF modes---including Independent---achieve near-Oracle
performance through ToU-aware dynamic $C$ vectors. The Federated and
ToM modes provide complementary coordination mechanisms (belief sharing
and phantom inference, respectively) that offer additional benefits
in scenarios where inter-agent anticipation is valuable. In extreme
climates (Montreal, Phoenix), where HVAC runs at capacity, all modes
converge to similar costs as the dominant cost driver leaves little
room for battery optimization differences.

The Federated mode's pattern is consistent with the theoretical prediction
from federated inference \cite{friston2024federated}: sharing posterior
beliefs (full probability distributions, not point estimates) enables
agents to form decentralized consensus about shared world states, with
the magnitude of the benefit depending on the degree of coordination
opportunity in the environment. The auditory A matrix formulation
(Section~\ref{sec:federated_mode}) ensures that social preferences
emerge naturally within the standard EFE computation
(Equation~\ref{eq:social_efe}), requiring no additional optimization
objective or hand-tuned coordination mechanism. The reliability parameter
(Equation~\ref{eq:reliability}) provides a principled mechanism for trust
calibration, allowing agents to discount unreliable reports.

The ToM mode provides an alternative path to coordination: rather than
communicating beliefs directly, each agent maintains a phantom model
of the other's decision process. This phantom-based approach implements
sophisticated inference \cite{pitliya2025theory}: the Battery Agent
predicts the Thermostat Agent's future HVAC decisions and incorporates
these predictions into step-dependent energy transition matrices within
its EFE computation. The custom EFE correctly includes the observation
entropy term $H[q(o|\pi)]$ alongside utility and ambiguity, matching
the standard pymdp decomposition. This approach requires no communication
channel, making it suitable for settings where inter-agent messaging
is unavailable or unreliable.

The predictive $B$ matrix approach (Section~\ref{sec:predictive_B})
demonstrates a principled way to incorporate forecast information into
the active inference framework. Rather than treating exogenous state
dynamics as unknown or static, agents update their transition
beliefs using available information---published tariff schedules,
occupancy calendars, and weather forecasts---at each time step. This
is consistent with the active inference principle that an agent should
maintain the most accurate generative model possible: forecast data
simply provides better priors on state transitions. The Gaussian
kernel for outdoor temperature (Equation~\ref{eq:predictive_B_temp})
naturally encodes forecast uncertainty, with the spread parameter
$\sigma$ reflecting confidence in the weather prediction.

The ToM mode's phantom-based architecture demonstrates how inter-agent
prediction can be integrated into the active inference framework. The
Battery Agent's phantom thermostat provides a probabilistic forecast of
HVAC activity, which enters the EFE computation through step-dependent
$B_{\text{energy}}$ matrices (Equation~\ref{eq:phantom_B}). This
construction is noteworthy because anticipatory coordination emerges
entirely within the standard EFE framework: no custom objective function
or additive cost penalty is required. The per-step ToU schedule within
the custom EFE gives the sophisticated agent full-horizon tariff
awareness, complementing the phantom's HVAC predictions.

From a broader perspective, the five coordination modes map onto a
spectrum from fully decentralized (Independent) to fully centralized
(Hierarchical), with the Aligned, Federated, and ToM modes occupying
intermediate positions. The Federated and ToM modes represent two
complementary approaches to inter-agent coordination: explicit belief
sharing and implicit phantom inference. Both achieve comparable
performance, but through different mechanisms---the Federated mode
requires a communication channel while the ToM mode requires shared
knowledge of the other agent's generative model structure. This
suggests that the coordination benefit arises from the agents'
ability to anticipate each other's behavior, regardless of the
specific mechanism. This finding is consistent with work on
collective active inference \cite{heins2024collective,heins2023spin}
and Theory of Mind through sophisticated inference
\cite{pitliya2025theory}.

The climate sensitivity analysis (Section~\ref{sec:climate}) demonstrates
that EcoNet generalizes across diverse climate conditions without
retraining---a direct consequence of active inference's online adaptation
through belief updating, in contrast to RL approaches that would require
re-training for each new climate. The agent adapts its behavior---for
example, prioritizing cooling in Phoenix summers and heating in Montreal
winters---through the standard belief-updating mechanism of active inference. The use of
real weather data from the IEM ASOS network provides confidence that
performance would be maintained under realistic operating conditions.

The uncertainty robustness analysis (Section~\ref{sec:uncertainty})
provides evidence that AIF agents degrade gracefully under forecast
noise. In Phoenix, where cooling demand dominates regardless of precise
temperature predictions, AIF costs are completely invariant to noise
levels up to $\sigma = 4$~$^{\circ}$C (0.0\% increase), while MPC
degrades by 3.8\%. This robustness arises from the generative model's
probabilistic structure: rather than optimizing against a single
forecast trajectory, the agent maintains beliefs over possible states
and selects policies that perform well across this distribution.

The RL comparison (Section~\ref{sec:rl_comparison}) highlights three
distinctive advantages of the active inference approach. First, AIF
achieves near-Oracle costs (2.9--4.0\% gap) with zero training,
while tabular Q-learning requires 20,000 episodes and still incurs
14.5--26.2\% higher costs. Second, AIF generalizes across climates
without retraining (0\% transfer degradation), while RL policies
overfit to training conditions (11.8\% degradation from London to
Phoenix). Third, AIF exhibits substantially lower variance across
seeds ($\pm$\$0.44 vs.\ $\pm$\$2.37), reflecting the stability of
model-based planning compared to value-function estimation. These
results suggest that model-based active inference is better suited
than model-free RL for energy management, where the physical dynamics
are well-understood and can be encoded directly in the generative model.

The validation results (Section~\ref{sec:validation}) confirm that
the simulation framework maintains physical consistency. This is
important because energy management systems that violate conservation
laws or constraint boundaries in simulation cannot be trusted for
deployment. The gamma sensitivity analysis further shows that performance
is robust to the choice of precision parameter within a reasonable
range.

Multiple studies suggest that HEMS and related energy management systems
have potential to reduce energy costs, conserve resources, reduce
greenhouse gas emissions, and increase grid stability and function
\cite{khabbazi2025,Mahapatra2022-zp,Martinez-2021,Tuomela-2021}.
To highlight one example, Gualandri and Kuzior \cite{Gualandri-2023}
demonstrate that increased adoption of HEMS could dramatically reduce
residential-energy consumption in Italy, which in their view could
help the country to achieve 2030 European energy targets. The degree
of future HEMS adoption depends of course on choices made by manufacturers,
utilities, consumer groups, end users, policymakers, and other stakeholders,
which in turn depends on the degree of cooperation between these entities
\cite{land14030566,Khafiso-2024}.

\subsection{Limitations}

Several limitations of the current study should be acknowledged.
First, the 2-hour time step discretization limits the model's ability
to capture fast dynamics such as HVAC cycling or sub-hourly ToU tariffs.
Finer temporal resolution would increase the state space and planning
horizon, potentially requiring approximate inference methods.
Second, the $B$ matrices for exogenous factors (outdoor temperature,
occupancy, ToU) are currently rebuilt from known schedules and forecasts
at each step; in a real deployment, forecast uncertainty would grow
with the prediction horizon and should be modeled explicitly.
Third, the 2-day baseline comparison (Table~\ref{tab:baseline}) uses
a single seed; the definitive multi-seed results are presented in
Tables~\ref{tab:coordination} and~\ref{tab:oracle_gap}.
Finally, the symmetric sophisticated mode (where both agents maintain
phantoms) is computationally expensive due to the expanded policy space
($3^6 = 729$ policies with 5 state factors); we report only the one-sided
ToM mode (Battery Agent phantom, standard policy space). Computational
optimization (e.g., policy pruning, amortized inference) would be needed
for full symmetric deployment.

\subsection{Future Work}

There are many possibilities for future work that could extend or
improve upon the current model. Like Nazemi et al. we could employ
a continuous state space and test the model using data generated from
real buildings or accurate digital twins. Although our aim is to coordinate
energy management across households, the current model only considers
management within a single building. Extension to neighborhoods or
building clusters would naturally expand our two-agent model to a
many-agent approach. The belief-sharing architecture presented here
provides a natural foundation for such scaling, as each agent need only
communicate with its immediate neighbors rather than a central coordinator
\cite{friston2024federated}. While our predictive $B$ matrices already
incorporate weather forecasts and known schedules, future efforts could
extend this to fully probabilistic multi-step forecasts, where forecast
uncertainty grows with the prediction horizon. More efficient methods for parameter and
structure learning are also possible, especially for learning complex
thermodynamic models of buildings. Renormalization group models or
tensor networks could be interesting here due to their capacity to
capture scale-invariance and compress data associated with high dimensional
functions, including probability functions \cite{Friston-2025,Roa-2024}.
The reliability mechanism could be extended to learn the auditory
A matrix itself, allowing agents to adaptively model each other's
communication patterns, a form of hierarchical active inference
\cite{pezzulo2018hierarchical,poppel2022resonating} applied to
social cognition. The ToM mode's phantom inference could be enriched
by allowing the phantom to learn from observation (i.e., updating
the phantom's beliefs based on observed energy consumption patterns),
creating a fully adaptive Theory of Mind.
Finally, it would be interesting to apply an active inference model
to infer the goals, preferences, and beliefs of households that largely
determine how they manage their energy devices. For example, a solar
battery inverter can be managed in numerous ways, for numerous purposes,
each of which could alter the voltages, currents, and power factors
seen at the point of common coupling with the grid. Given a history
of device management, as evidenced by the numerical readings available
to grid operators, what model best explains the management practices
of a household, and so also its expected energy impacts on the grid?

\section{Conclusion}

We have presented EcoNet, a multi-agent active inference framework for
household energy management that unifies perception, planning, learning,
and coordination under the free energy principle. The system coordinates
a Thermostat Agent and a Battery Agent to balance conflicting objectives
---energy cost, thermal comfort, and greenhouse gas emissions---under
uncertainty. Key contributions include: (i) predictive $B$ matrices that
incorporate forecast information into the generative model; (ii) a
Theory of Mind coordination mode based on sophisticated inference
\cite{pitliya2025theory}, where agents maintain phantom models of each
other's decision processes for anticipatory planning; (iii) a federated
belief sharing mode for decentralized consensus; and (iv) a battery
model with explicit round-trip efficiency losses ($\eta = 0.9$ one-way).

Experimental results demonstrate that EcoNet reduces costs relative to
unmanaged operation and rule-based control, generalizes across diverse
climate conditions without retraining, and achieves coordination benefits
through both phantom inference (ToM) and belief sharing (Federated). The
comparison with Oracle, MPC, and RL baselines highlights active
inference's distinctive advantages: online learning without offline
training, principled uncertainty quantification, and interpretable
preference structures through $C$ vectors. Uncertainty robustness
experiments confirm that AIF maintains stable costs under forecast
noise (0\% degradation in Phoenix at $\sigma = 4$~$^{\circ}$C, vs.\
3.8\% for MPC), and a systematic RL comparison shows that AIF achieves
near-Oracle performance with zero training while RL requires 20,000+
episodes and still incurs 14--26\% higher costs with 11.8\% cross-climate
transfer degradation.

The framework provides a foundation for scaling to multi-household
neighborhoods and integrating additional DER devices, leveraging both
the belief-sharing and phantom-inference architectures for decentralized
coordination.
